text.title: rust-tokio-async Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for rust-tokio-async. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Before implementing any concurrent async code, classify the workload into one of four classes and pick the corresponding primitive. Do not use a heavier primitive when a lighter one fits.
rule.rationale: Choosing buffer_unordered for two parallel futures is over-engineering; choosing try_join! for 10,000 items is an OOM risk. The right primitive matches the workload size and semantics.

rule.statement: Use tokio::try_join! to run a fixed, small number of futures in parallel and collect all results, short-circuiting on the first error. Do not use it for collections with unknown or large N.
rule.rationale: try_join! resolves all futures simultaneously. For N > ~20 simultaneous futures it may overload connection pools. For N known at compile time it is the cleanest syntax.

rule.statement: Wrap any CPU-heavy operation (Argon2, bcrypt, regex compilation, image processing, zip compression) in tokio::task::spawn_blocking. Await the result with .await?? to propagate both the join error (panic) and the inner Result.
rule.rationale: CPU-heavy futures block the async executor thread, starving all other tasks on that thread. spawn_blocking runs the closure on a dedicated blocking thread pool, keeping the async threads free.

rule.statement: Process large collections with futures::stream::buffer_unordered(N) combined with tokio::sync::Semaphore for precise concurrency control. Use Semaphore::acquire_owned() so the permit lives as long as the task, not as long as a borrow.
rule.rationale: buffer_unordered(N) limits in-flight futures to N, but the Semaphore adds an explicit capacity token that can be inspected, logged, and adjusted without changing the stream topology.

rule.statement: Use tokio::task::JoinSet instead of Vec<JoinHandle<T>> for groups of spawned tasks. JoinSet cancels all remaining tasks on drop, preventing leaks when the parent scope exits early.
rule.rationale: A Vec<JoinHandle> requires explicit abort() loops on early exit; forgetting them leaves tasks running indefinitely. JoinSet cancellation is automatic and unconditional on drop.

rule.statement: Wrap every external IO call in tokio::time::timeout. There is no safe default for a missing timeout — every external call must have an explicit one.
rule.rationale: A hung external service with no timeout holds a Tokio task thread indefinitely. Under load this exhausts the worker thread pool and stalls the entire runtime.

rule.statement: When using tokio::select! with non-cancellation-safe futures (e.g., AsyncReadExt::read_to_end, AsyncWriteExt::write_all), document the cancel-safety status of each branch with a comment. Cancelling a non-safe future at an arbitrary .await point drops partial state silently.
rule.rationale: tokio::select! drops the losing future at the next .await point. If the future has partially written state (a half-flushed buffer, an uncommitted transaction), the partial state is silently lost with no error.

rule.statement: Never hold std::sync::MutexGuard across a .await point. Use tokio::sync::Mutex only when the critical section itself must .await. For most cases, prefer message-passing (tokio::sync::mpsc) over shared mutable state.
rule.rationale: std::sync::MutexGuard is not Send. Holding it across .await causes a compile error if the type is caught, or a deadlock if the executor tries to move the future to another thread. The compiler does not always catch this; clippy::await_holding_lock does.
